text: I am afraid we can not deliver it by Friday.
Unfortunately, a discount is not possible.
We are not able to offer that at this time.
I understand, but the timeline is fixed.
Patrícia, I need the feature by Friday, a discount, and 24/7 support. Can you confirm all three?
Thank you for being honest, Mark. I am afraid we can not deliver the feature by Friday, but I can give you a firm date.
That is not what I wanted to hear. What about the thirty percent discount?
Unfortunately, a discount that size is not something we can offer. However, I can add three months of premium support.
And the 24-hour engineer? My team really expects it.
We are not able to provide 24/7 support at this time. What I can do is set a clear escalation path for urgent issues.

Hi Patricia, it is Mark again, leaving a quick voicemail because I have not heard back yet, and I will be honest, my team is getting impatient. I know I am asking for a lot in my email, but please understand the pressure I am under. My CEO brought up the contract again this morning, and I did not have a good answer. I am not trying to be difficult here. If you genuinely cannot deliver the feature by Friday, I would rather you tell me that now than promise something and miss it again. The same goes for the discount and the support hours. I respect a straight, honest answer far more than a vague yes. So please, when you reply, be clear about what you can and cannot do, and give me a realistic plan I can take back to my CEO. Thanks, and talk soon.

Hi Patricia, it is Sofia from the customer success team, leaving you a short note before you reply to that tough email from Mark. I have handled a lot of these, so let me share how I would approach it. First, never start your reply with a flat no, because it sounds cold and it invites more pushback. Begin by thanking him and showing that you understand his frustration. Then be honest about the limitations. If you cannot deliver the feature by Friday, say so clearly, but soften it with I am afraid we are not able to do that this week. Whenever you say no to one thing, offer an alternative for it, like a firm delivery date or extra premium support instead of the discount. That way he hears a plan, not just a wall. And remember to reassure him that his account matters to us. A calm, tactful no protects the relationship far better than a panicked yes you cannot keep. You have got this. Call me if you want to talk it through.
I am afraid we can not deliver it by Friday.
Unfortunately, a discount is not possible.
We are not able to offer that at this time.
I understand, but the timeline is fixed.
Let me suggest an alternative that works for both sides.
Unfortunately, that discount is not possible.
